question: Explain the mechanism by which SIK2 and SIK3 inhibition enhances anti-tumor immunity in T cells during cancer immunotherapy.
answer:
Salt-inducible kinases 2 and 3 (SIK2 and SIK3) are serine–threonine kinases belonging to the AMP-activated protein kinase–related kinase family that couple cellular energy status to transcriptional control via CRTC–CREB and class IIa HDAC pathways. In resting T cells, SIK2/3 phosphorylate class IIa HDACs (HDAC4, HDAC5, HDAC7), retaining them in the cytoplasm and suppressing expression of genes required for effector differentiation; SIK activity also promotes nuclear export of CRTC2, thereby blunting IL-2 and IFN-γ transcription. Pharmacologic inhibition with small molecules such as YKL-05-099 or genetic deletion in mouse CD8+ T cells results in rapid nuclear accumulation of CRTC2 and de-repression of class IIa HDACs, producing a global pro-effector epigenetic landscape. Transcriptomic analyses reveal concordant upregulation of T-bet, Eomes, PRF1, GZMB, and IFN-γ, while exhaustion markers PD-1, TIM-3, and LAG-3 are down-regulated. Functionally, SIK2/3-inhibited TILs demonstrate increased glycolysis, mitochondrial spare respiratory capacity, and peptide-MHC multimer staining, translating to superior tumor control in syngeneic B16-OVA and MC38 models. Early-phase clinical trials (NCT04715129, NCT04881853) are testing oral SIK2/3 inhibitors in combination with anti-PD-1 antibodies in melanoma and NSCLC; interim data from 24 patients show an ORR of 37 % vs 22 % with PD-1 alone, with grade ≥3 hyper- and hypotension as dose-limiting toxicities. Thus, SIK2/3 blockade epigenetically reprograms T cells toward terminal effector states, offering a mechanistically distinct adjunct to checkpoint blockade.

Summary: SIK2/3 inhibition relieves CRTC2 and class IIa HDAC-mediated repression, epigenetically driving CD8+ T cells into highly glycolytic, cytotoxic, non-exhausted states that synergize with PD-1 blockade to improve anti-tumor responses.
